fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get this Rust builder codegen library wrapped up for the release. It's basically a proc-macro thing that auto-generates those typed construction APIs for structs so devs don't have to write all that boilerplate themselves. The big thing is it needs to be compile-time safe — like, if someone forgets a required field or tries to set a field they're not supposed to touch, it should blow up at compile time, not runtime. We had that issue with the old approach where hidden internal fields were accidentally settable from outside, which caused some really annoying prod bugs last quarter.

Also, the library needs to handle all the Rust-specific stuff — borrows, mutable borrows, generics, lifetimes, the works. And there's that thing we discussed about how some field names conflict with Rust keywords (similar to how we handled it in the auth module refactor), make sure that's covered properly.

There should also be support for optional fields with shorthand setters, boolean flag fields, defaults that depend on other field values, and the ability to clone a partially-built record and finish it in different ways. Custom names for the builder start/finalize methods would be nice too. Basically take the full spec and make sure all those construction patterns work end to end through the test harness we already have set up.

trigger_keywords: required fields order
field supply order doesn't matter
any order for required fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Required fields must be settable in any order. The builder uses a type-state pattern (phantom type parameters tracking which fields have been set) so that `set_left().set_right()` and `set_right().set_left()` both produce the same final `Pair { left, right }` record. The contract key is `required_fields` and the record name is `Pair`. Output format: `record=Pair
left=<v>
right=<v>
`.

trigger_keywords: borrowed values lifetime
reference fields in builder
independent vs bounded lifetime
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The builder must support records with borrowed fields. When `bounded` is false the record is `BorrowedPair` with independent lifetimes. The contract key is `borrowed_values`. Output: `record=BorrowedPair
left=<v>
right=<v>
bounded=false
`. The builder must correctly propagate lifetime annotations so the borrow checker is satisfied at the call site.

trigger_keywords: mutable borrow through record
mutate original via constructed struct
mutable reference field mutation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a record stores `&mut` fields, writing through those fields after construction must mutate the original variables. Contract key: `mutable_borrows`. The test multiplies each original value and checks the originals changed. Output: `left_after=<left*left_mul>\nright_after=<right*right_mul>\n`. The builder must not copy or move the mutable references.

trigger_keywords: generic type parameters in builder
monomorphized builder
caller-chosen field types
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The builder must support records with generic field types `<L, R>`. The generated builder is also generic and the type parameters are inferred from the values passed in. Contract key: `generic_fields`, record name `GenericPair`. Output: `record=GenericPair
left=<v>
right=<v>
`.

trigger_keywords: converting setter input type
field accepts compatible type
Into conversion on setter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A field setter can accept a value of a different but compatible input type and store the converted value. For `small_unsigned_integer` input type the setter accepts `u8` and stores as the field type via `Into`. Contract key: `converting_setter`, record `ConvertedValue`. Output: `record=ConvertedValue\nvalue=<v>\n`.

trigger_keywords: optional field shorthand
Some wrapping skipped
present value without Option wrapper
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `Option<T>` fields the builder can expose a setter that accepts `T` directly (unwrapped) and internally wraps it in `Some`. The `option_order` parameter `present_then_convert` means the shorthand setter applies `Into` conversion before wrapping. Contract key: `optional_shorthand`, record `OptionalValue`. Output: `record=OptionalValue\nvalue=present:<v>\n`.

trigger_keywords: boolean flag default false
flag setter no argument
enable flag without value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A `bool` field annotated as a flag defaults to `false` and is set to `true` by calling a zero-argument setter (no value passed). Contract key: `boolean_flag`, record `BooleanFlag`. When `enabled` is true in the test input the flag setter is called. Output: `record=BooleanFlag\nenabled=true\n`.

trigger_keywords: constant default value
omitted field uses default
optional during construction with fallback
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Fields with `#[builder(default = <expr>)]` are optional; when the caller omits them the constant expression is used. Contract key: `constant_defaults`, record `Defaults`. Defaults: `maybe=None` (prints `absent`), `number=10`, `items=vec![20,30,40]` (prints `[20, 30, 40]`). Output: `record=Defaults
maybe=absent
number=10
items=[20, 30, 40]
`.

trigger_keywords: dependent default expression
default reads another field
field default depends on sibling field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A default expression for one field may reference a previously resolved field using `#[builder(default_code = "...")]` or similar. Contract key: `dependent_defaults`, record `DependentDefaults`. With no overrides: `maybe=absent`, `number=10`, `items=[number, 30, 40]` → `[10, 30, 40]`. Output: `record=DependentDefaults
maybe=absent
number=10
items=[10, 30, 40]
`.

trigger_keywords: hidden field skipped from builder
field not exposed as setter
skip field in construction interface
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A field marked with `#[builder(skip)]` or equivalent is excluded from the builder's public API but still appears in the final record using its default. Contract key: `skipped_fields`, record `SkippedFields`. Fields: `hidden_default=0` (skipped, default 0), `visible=<v>` (caller-supplied), `dependent_hidden=visible*2` (skipped, depends on visible). Output: `record=SkippedFields
hidden_default=0
visible=1
dependent_hidden=2
`.

trigger_keywords: record-level field policy
default policy for all fields
per-record builder attribute override
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A record-level attribute can set a default policy (e.g., default value or setter shape) that applies to all fields unless individually overridden. Contract key: `field_default_value_policy`, record `FieldDefaultValues`. Fields: `first=12` (default), `required_text=<caller>` (required, no default), `third=13` (default). Output: `record=FieldDefaultValues
first=12
required_text=bla
third=13
`.

trigger_keywords: clone partial builder
branch from incomplete builder
copy builder mid-construction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A builder with `Clone` support can be cloned mid-construction (before all required fields are set) and each clone completed independently. Contract key: `clone_partial_builder`, record `CloneBranch`. Fields: `branch` (index), `x` (seed, shared), `y` (per-branch), `z` (default string `"default"`). Two records are built and concatenated. Output: `record=CloneBranch
branch=0
x=<seed>
y=<branch0>
z=default
record=CloneBranch
branch=1
x=<seed>
y=<branch1>
z=default
`.

trigger_keywords: reserved word field name
Rust keyword as struct field
field named function or type or enum
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Fields whose names are Rust reserved keywords (e.g., `type`, `fn`, `enum`, `union`) must be handled with raw identifiers (`r#type` etc.) internally while the generated API uses the plain name. Contract key: `reserved_word_fields`, record `ReservedWords`. Fields: `function_value` (required i32), `type_value` (Option, absent), `enum_value` (Option<()>, default `Some(())` → `present:unit`), `union_text` (required String). Output: `record=ReservedWords
function_value=1
type_value=absent
enum_value=present:unit
union_text=two
`.

trigger_keywords: multi-input setter transformation
setter takes multiple arguments
composite value from multiple caller inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A field setter can be configured to accept multiple caller-supplied arguments that are combined into one stored value. Contract key: `transformed_field`, record `TransformedField`. The `point` field is set by passing `x` and `y` separately; they are stored as a composite. Output: `record=TransformedField
point.x=1
point.y=2
`.

trigger_keywords: custom finalize method name
custom start method name
rename builder or build method
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The builder start function (default `builder()`) and finalize function (default `build()`) can be renamed via attributes. Contract keys: `custom_finalize_name` (record `CustomFinalize`, custom build-method name) and `custom_start_name` (record `CustomStart`, custom builder-start name). Both produce `record=<Name>\nvalue=<v>\n`.

trigger_keywords: compile time rejection hidden field
setting hidden field is compile error
error category for invalid builder usage
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attempting to call a setter for a skipped/hidden field must be a compile-time error. The test harness represents this as a contract query that returns a normalized error category. Contract key: `compile_rejection`, invalid action `set_hidden_field`. Output: `error=hidden_field_not_settable\nfield=hidden\n`. No runtime panic is acceptable; the error must be structural.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled it in the auth module refactor
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The reserved-keyword field handling described in feature13_reserved_words_as_fields.json — using raw identifiers (r#type, r#fn, etc.) internally in the generated code so that field names that are Rust keywords remain valid identifiers in the macro output.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that issue with the old approach where hidden internal fields were accidentally settable from outside
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 16 (feature16_compile_time_rejections.json): the `compile_rejection` contract with `set_hidden_field` action, which must produce a compile-time error category `hidden_field_not_settable` rather than a runtime failure.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the test harness we already have set up
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON-driven execution adapter in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ that dispatches on the `contract` field of each input to call the appropriate builder demonstration function and serializes the result to the expected newline-delimited key=value output format.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full context on from my side — you'd need to dig into the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the PRD spec to get the precise behavior. Can you check there and let me know if something still looks ambiguous?

feature10_skipped_fields.json.description: A field can be intentionally unavailable as a caller input when it has a default. Such fields are still present in the final record and may use defaults that depend on supplied fields.

feature11_field_default_policies.json.description: Default and input-shaping policies can be declared for all fields of a record, while individual fields may opt out or override those policies.

feature12_clone_partial_builder.json.description: A partially configured construction plan can be copied and completed in different ways as long as the already supplied values support copying.

cases.expected_output:
record=CloneBranch
branch=0
x=1
y=2
z=default
record=CloneBranch
branch=1
x=1
y=3
z=default

feature13_reserved_words_as_fields.json.description: A record can include fields whose textual names are reserved by the host language; the generated construction interface still assigns caller-provided and defaulted values correctly.

feature14_transformed_field_input.json.description: A field may be assigned by a transformation that accepts multiple caller values and stores one composite value in the constructed record.

feature15_customized_entrypoints_and_out.description: The generated construction API can expose custom names for starting construction, completing construction, or naming the construction-plan type. A completed inner record may also be converted into an enclosing output type.

feature16_compile_time_rejections.json.description: The construction API rejects invalid programs such as supplying a hidden field, defining a hidden field without a default, or copying a partially configured plan that contains a non-copyable value. Errors are reported as normalized contract categories.

feature1_required_fields_any_order.json.description: A record with two required integer fields is constructed only from caller-supplied values; changing the order of supplied fields must not change which final field receives each value.

feature2_borrowed_values.json.description: A record may contain borrowed input values. Construction preserves the referenced values for fields that use independent or bounded lifetimes.

feature3_mutable_borrows.json.description: When a record stores mutable borrowed values, mutating through the constructed record changes the original input locations.

feature4_generic_fields.json.description: A record whose field types are chosen by the caller can be constructed with concrete values and preserves those values in the final record.

feature5_converting_setters.json.description: A field can accept a value of a compatible input type and store it as the field type after conversion.

feature6_optional_value_shorthand.json.description: A caller can provide the present value for an optional field without wrapping it. The constructed record stores the value as present.

feature7_boolean_flag_shorthand.json.description: A boolean field configured as a flag defaults to false and becomes true when the caller includes the flag action.

feature8_constant_defaults.json.description: Fields marked with defaults are optional during construction. When omitted, they use their configured default values; provided values override only the corresponding fields.

feature9_dependent_defaults.json.description: A default expression for one field may read another field that has already been resolved. If the dependency is overridden, the dependent default reflects the override unless it too is overridden.
